1: Psalm 120 opens the maAlot collection — fifteen Songs of Ascents (Pss 120-134) sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three great pilgrim festivals: Passover, Weeks (Pentecost), and Tabernacles (Exodus 23:14-17; Deuteronomy 16:16). The word maAlot (steps, ascents) connects to: (1) the physical ascent of the roads to Jerusalem, which sits at ~760m elevation; (2) the 15 semicircular steps between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel in the temple, on which the Mishnah (Middot Psalms 2:5) says the Levites sang these 15 psalms; (3) the "going up" (alah) from Babylonian exile (Ezra 7:9). The collection opens not at Jerusalem but in alienation — the pilgrim's starting point is foreignness and slander. The opening cry of distress that receives a divine answer frames the whole pilgrimage: the journey begins in a place where prayer is the only available help, and moves toward the place where prayer finds its home.
2: "Lying lips and deceitful tongue (lashon remiyyah)" — in the ANE honor-shame social system, verbal warfare was the primary mechanism of social destruction. False accusation, slander in court, perjured testimony, and malicious rumor could strip a person of honor, property, legal standing, and community belonging. The tongue was the weapon most feared because it was hardest to defend against. The Levitical system recognized this urgency: Leviticus 19:16 forbids slander; Deuteronomy 19:16-21 requires that a false witness receive the penalty intended for the accused. The psalmist's prayer for rescue from "lying lips" is not merely personal — it is a prayer for the covenant community's justice system to operate. The opening of the pilgrimage collection with the problem of the lying tongue situates the whole Psalter of Ascents in the context of an unjust social world from which the pilgrim seeks relief at Zion.
3: "What will come to you, and what more will be done to you, O deceitful tongue?" — the rhetorical question poses a verdict, which v4 then supplies. The structure is a mock-interrogation of the tongue itself, treating the organ of deception as the defendant in a court proceeding. The psalmist does not threaten personal retaliation — he poses the divine verdict in the form of a question that the deceitful tongue cannot answer, since the nature of the lying tongue is to be silent before the truth it has been evading. In the ancient legal context, the question addressed to the offending party before sentencing was a formal judicial move; the psalmist invokes this court form against the tongue that has wronged him, appealing the case to the divine court.
4: "A warrior's sharp arrows, fired with glowing coals of broom wood (ritmim)" — the sentence on the deceitful tongue uses military and thermal imagery. The arrow (hez) in the OT is frequently the weapon of divine judgment (Psalms 7:13; Psalms 38:2; Psalms 64:7; Job 6:4; Lamentations 3:12-13). "Broom wood coals (retem)" — the Retama raetam (white broom plant) of the Negev desert produces the hottest-burning, longest-retaining coals known to the ancient world; they remain hot even when appearing extinguished. Elijah rested under a broom tree during his flight from Jezebel (1 Kings 19:4-5). The combination of sharp arrows and glowing broom coals gives a punishment that is both precise (arrows striking a specific target) and sustained (coals that hold their heat) — the intensity and duration of the judgment matches the intensity and duration of the damage the lying tongue has caused.
5: "Woe to me, that I must live as a foreigner in Meshech (mashak) and make my dwelling among the tents of Kedar" — Meshech (Genesis 10:2) was a people of northern Anatolia, between the Black Sea and Caspian Sea; Kedar (Genesis 25:13) was an Arab nomadic confederacy of the Syrian-Arabian desert. These two groups are geographically as far apart as the psalmist could name — separated by thousands of kilometers. Used together as a merism, they represent the furthest extremities of the hostile pagan world: not literal geography (one cannot simultaneously live in Anatolia and Arabia) but the symbolic statement "I am living as far from Zion as it is possible to be, among people as unlike the covenant community as exist." Isaiah uses Meshech (Isaiah 66:19) and Kedar (Isaiah 21:16-17; Psalms 42:11) in similar "ends of the earth" contexts. The pilgrim who has not yet reached Jerusalem is, in the psalm's geography, still in "Meshech and Kedar" — exile from covenant community and the divine presence.
6: "My soul has lived too long (rabbat shakenah-lah nafshi) among those who hate peace" — the excessive length of the sojourn is the point: not a brief diplomatic visit to hostile territory but a prolonged, wearing residence. "Hate peace (sone shalom)" — shalom encompasses the full covenant vision: communal wellbeing, right relationships, material sufficiency, and divine-human reconciliation. Those who hate shalom are those whose entire orientation is toward disruption, conflict, and the destruction of covenant community order. The phrase identifies the deepest category of the psalm's adversaries: not merely those who are angry or competitive with the psalmist personally, but those whose fundamental disposition is against the ordering principle of creation and covenant.
7: "I am for peace (ani shalom) — but the moment I open my mouth, they want war" — the compressed syntax carries maximum weight: in Hebrew, ani shalom ("I — peace") identifies the entire person with the concept, not merely a preference. "I am peace" is the full identification of self with covenant shalom. The moment of speech is the moment of maximum vulnerability — the psalmist opens his mouth (to speak truth, to seek covenant resolution, to offer peace) and the response is immediate hostility (laqerab, "for battle"). This closing verse of the opening pilgrim psalm frames the entire collection: the journey to Jerusalem is a journey from a world of chronic hostility toward a place where covenant shalom is embodied and practiced — where the lips that are lying in Meshech become the lips of praise at Zion's gates.
